request.question: Invasion Attack included title matches by the Japanese sumo wrestler from what Hawaiian city?

title: Takanonami Sadahiro
Endō Shōta
Akebono Tarō
Aminishiki Ryūji
Kotoōshū Katsunori
Jōkōryū Takayuki
Yamamotoyama Ryūta
Invasion Attack (2013)
Kasugafuji Akihiro
Kaiketsu Masateru
sentences: Takanonami Sadahiro (born Sadahiro Namioka October 27, 1971 – June 20, 2015) was a Japanese sumo wrestler and coach from Aomori.
He held sumo's second highest rank of "ōzeki" from 1994 until 2000.
He won two tournament championships, and was a runner-up in eight others.
He was a sumo coach from his retirement in 2004 until his death in 2015 at the age of 43.
Endō Shōta (遠藤 聖大 ) , (born October 19, 1990) is a professional sumo wrestler from Ishikawa Prefecture, Japan.
After a successful amateur career, he turned professional in March 2013, making the top "makuuchi" division that September.
His highest rank has been "maegashira" 1, and he has two special prizes for Fighting Spirit and Technique, and three gold stars for defeating "yokozuna."
He was runner-up in the September 2016 tournament.
He wrestles for Oitekaze stable.
He is extremely popular with Japanese sumo fans and has been regarded as one of the most promising home-grown wrestlers in sumo.
Akebono Tarō (曙 太郎 , Akebono Tarō , born Chadwick Haheo Rowan; 8 May 1969) is an American-born Japanese professional wrestler and former sumo wrestler from Waimānalo, Hawaii.
Joining the professional sport in Japan in 1988, he was trained by pioneering Hawaiian sumo wrestler Takamiyama and rose swiftly up the rankings, reaching the top division in 1990.

After two consecutive "yusho" or tournament championships in November 1992 and January 1993 he made history by becoming the first non-Japanese-born wrestler ever to reach "yokozuna", the highest rank in sumo.
Aminishiki Ryūji (born October 3, 1978 as Ryūji Suginomori) is a Japanese sumo wrestler.
He made his professional debut in 1997 and reached the top "makuuchi" division in 2000.
He has earned eleven special prizes and won eight "kinboshi" or gold stars for defeating "yokozuna" during his career, which for some time was twice as many as any other active wrestler.
He has twice been runner-up in a tournament.
The highest rank he has reached is "sekiwake".
He is in the all-time top ten for a number of sumo records, including most top division wins, most top division appearances and most tournaments ranked in the top division.
He wrestles for Isegahama stable.

Kotoōshū Katsunori (琴欧洲 勝紀) (legal name: Karoyan Andō, born February 19, 1983 as Kaloyan Stefanov Mahlyanov, Bulgarian: Калоян Стефанов Махлянов , in Dzhulyunitsa, Veliko Tarnovo Province, Bulgaria) is a former professional sumo wrestler or "rikishi".
He made his debut in 2002, reaching the top division just two years later.
In 2005 he reached the rank of "ōzeki" or 'champion', the second-highest level in the sumo ranking system behind only "yokozuna."
On May 24, 2008, Kotoōshū made history by becoming the first European sumo wrestler to win an Emperor's Cup.
He was one of the longest serving "ōzeki" in sumo history, holding the rank for 47 consecutive tournaments until November 2013.
In January 2014 Kotoōshū obtained Japanese citizenship, a requirement of becoming an elder in the Japan Sumo Association, and he announced his retirement during the following tournament in March.
In April 2017 he opened his own training stable, Naruto.
Jōkōryū Takayuki (born August 7, 1988 as Takayuki Sakuma), formerly known as Sakumayama Takayuki, is a Japanese sumo wrestler.
He made his professional debut in 2011 after a successful college career in sumo.
He currently holds the record for the most consecutive wins since entering professional sumo, and for the fastest rise to the top "makuuchi" division from the lowest "jonokuchi" division.
His highest rank has been "komusubi", which he reached in September 2014.
However, because of injuries he has fallen down the rankings, reaching a low of "sandanme" 23 in November 2016.

Ryūichi Yamamoto (山本 龍一 , Yamamoto Ryūichi , born May 8, 1984) , known by his "shikona" Yamamotoyama Ryūta (山本山 龍太 ) , or simply Yama, is a Japanese retired sumo wrestler from the city of Saitama in Saitama Prefecture.
He made his professional debut in January 2007, and reached the top "makuuchi" division in January 2009.
His highest rank was "maegashira" 9.
At 265 kg , Yamamotoyama is the heaviest Japanese-born sumo wrestler in history, and is also thought to be the heaviest Japanese person ever.
In April 2011, he was told to retire by the Japan Sumo Association after he and several other wrestlers were found to be involved in match-fixing.
He now lives in Los Angeles and participates in sumo exhibitions and amateur tournaments, often alongside Ulambayaryn Byambajav.
Invasion Attack was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).
The event took place on April 7, 2013, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan.
The event featured nine matches, five of which were contested for championships.

As part of the "invasion" theme, the event featured title matches involving championships from Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre (CMLL) and the National Wrestling Alliance (NWA) as well as participation from outside workers Akebono and Bob Sapp.
It was the first event under the Invasion Attack name.
Kasugafuji Akihiro (February 20, 1966 – March 9, 2017), born as Shoki Iwanaga, was a Japanese sumo wrestler and coach from Oshika, Miyagi.
He was an active wrestler in professional sumo from 1981 until 1996, reaching a highest rank of "maegashira" 1.
After his retirement he re-established the Kasugayama stable in 1997 and trained his own wrestlers.

He left the Japan Sumo Association in 2012 after an expenses scandal, and was involved with a legal dispute in 2013 with his successor as head of Kasugayama stable which was not resolved until shortly before his death in 2017.
Kaiketsu Masateru (Japanese: 魁傑 將晃, born Teruyuki Nishimori; February 16, 1948 – May 18, 2014) was a Japanese sumo wrestler, who reached the second highest rank of "ōzeki" on two separate occasions.
He also won two top division tournament championships.
After his retirement in 1979 he became a coach under the name of Hanaregoma-oyakata and established Hanaregoma stable.
He was also chairman of the Japan Sumo Association from 2010 to 2012.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

Invasion Attack was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).

response.hop1_query: Invasion Attack included title matches by the Japanese sumo wrestler from what Hawaiian city?
hop1_docs: Akebono Tarō | Akebono Tarō (曙 太郎 , Akebono Tarō , born Chadwick Haheo Rowan; 8 May 1969) is an American-born Japanese professional wrestler and former sumo wrestler from Waimānalo, Hawaii.  Joining the professional sport in Japan in 1988, he was trained by pioneering Hawaiian sumo wrestler Takamiyama and rose swiftly up the rankings, reaching the top division in 1990.  After two consecutive "yusho" or tournament championships in November 1992 and January 1993 he made history by becoming the first non-Japanese-born wrestler ever to reach "yokozuna", the highest rank in sumo.

Yamamotoyama Ryūta | Ryūichi Yamamoto (山本 龍一 , Yamamoto Ryūichi , born May 8, 1984) , known by his "shikona" Yamamotoyama Ryūta (山本山 龍太 ) , or simply Yama, is a Japanese retired sumo wrestler from the city of Saitama in Saitama Prefecture.  He made his professional debut in January 2007, and reached the top "makuuchi" division in January 2009.  His highest rank was "maegashira" 9.  At 265 kg , Yamamotoyama is the heaviest Japanese-born sumo wrestler in history, and is also thought to be the heaviest Japanese person ever.  In April 2011, he was told to retire by the Japan Sumo Association after he and several other wrestlers were found to be involved in match-fixing.  He now lives in Los Angeles and participates in sumo exhibitions and amateur tournaments, often alongside Ulambayaryn Byambajav.

NJPW Invasion Tour 2011 | NJPW Invasion Tour 2011: Attack on East Coast was the first American tour run by Japanese professional wrestling promotion New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW) in May 2011.  The tour was hosted by Jersey All Pro Wrestling (JAPW) and featured all of NJPW's top wrestlers, matches for three of the promotion's four championships and the introduction of a new fifth title.

Magaki stable | Magaki stable (間垣部屋 , Magaki beya ) was a stable of sumo wrestlers, formerly one of the Nishonoseki group of stables.  Wakanohana Kanji II, the 56th Yokozuna in sumo history, re-established the stable in 1983.  Its first wrestler to reach the top "makuuchi" division was the Hawaiian born Yamato in 1997, followed by Gojōrō and Wakanojō, also in 1997.  However the stable has had less success in recent years.  Russian "maegashira" Wakanohō was thrown out of sumo in 2008 after being accused of cannabis possession, charges which were eventually dropped.  In 2011, its highest ranked wrestler Wakatenrō was forced to retire because of accusations of match-fixing which he admitted to after being banned from competition.

Kasugaō Katsumasa | Kasugaō Katsumasa 春日王 克昌 (born July 1, 1977 as Kim Sung Tak 김성택) is a former sumo wrestler from Incheon, South Korea.  He was the first sumo wrestler to reach the top "makuuchi" division officially representing South Korea.  (Several other top wrestlers in the past have hid a Korean or half-Korean background).  He joined sumo in 1998, making the top division for the first time in 2003.  His highest rank was "maegashira" 3.  In September 2009, he acquired Japanese citizenship.  In April 2011 he was ordered to retire by the Japan Sumo Association after an investigation found him guilty of match-fixing.

Konishiki Yasokichi | Konishiki Yasokichi (小錦八十吉 , Konishiki Yasokichi , born Saleva'a Fuauli Atisano'e; December 31, 1963) is a Hawaiian-born Japanese-Samoan former sumo wrestler.  He was the first non-Japanese-born wrestler to reach "ōzeki", the second-highest rank in the sport.  During his career he won the top division championship on three occasions and came close to becoming the first foreign-born grand champion, or "yokozuna", prompting a debate as to whether a foreigner could have the necessary cultural understanding to be acceptable in sumo's ultimate rank.  At a peak weight of 287 kg he was also the heaviest "rikishi" ever in sumo, earning him the nicknames "Meat Bomb" and "Dump Truck".

Toyonishiki Kiichiro | Toyonishiki Kiichiro (born Harley Ozaki, 3 February 1920 – 26 September 1998) was a Japanese-American sumo wrestler from Pierce, Colorado who was one of the first foreign-born wrestlers to reach the top "makuuchi" division.  He made his professional debut in January 1938 and reached "makuuchi" in May 1944, nearly 25 years before the more well known Hawaiian born Takamiyama Daigoro.  However, he fought in only one tournament in the top division before being drafted into the Imperial Japanese Army against his wishes, and he was never returned to sumo.  He remained in Japan after the war, and ran an inn later in life.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Akebono Tarō — (Akebono Tarō) "from Waimānalo, Hawaii." 
  - Konishiki Yasokichi — (Konishiki Yasokichi) "Hawaiian-born" (no city given).
  - Yamamotoyama Ryūta — (Yamamotoyama Ryūta) "from the city of Saitama" (not Hawaiian).
  - Magaki stable / Yamato — (Magaki stable) "the Hawaiian born Yamato" (no city given).
  - Invasion Attack event/page — NOT present in supplied passages.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - The phrase "the Japanese sumo wrestler" could refer to multiple Hawaii-linked wrestlers (Akebono, Konishiki, Yamato); passages do not identify which one wrestled at Invasion Attack.
  - No passage confirms that any of these wrestlers had title matches at an event named Invasion Attack.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - The Invasion Attack event page or match card showing which wrestler(s) had title matches.
  - Confirmation linking the named wrestler who appeared at Invasion Attack to their hometown city in Hawaii (if not already specified).

- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. "Invasion Attack (NJPW) event" — event page / match card listing title matches and participants.
  2. "Invasion Attack Akebono" or "Akebono Invasion Attack match" — to confirm Akebono's participation/title matches.
  3. "Akebono Tarō" — biography (to confirm hometown: Waimānalo, Hawaii) — already present but re-check for event linkage.
  4. "Konishiki Yasokichi Invasion Attack" and "Yamato (sumo) biography" — if event page names a different Hawaiian-linked wrestler, retrieve their city.
response.hop2_query: Invasion Attack (NJPW) match card title matches Akebono Konishiki Yamato
hop2_docs: Invasion Attack (2013) | Invasion Attack was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 7, 2013, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan.  The event featured nine matches, five of which were contested for championships.  As part of the "invasion" theme, the event featured title matches involving championships from Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre (CMLL) and the National Wrestling Alliance (NWA) as well as participation from outside workers Akebono and Bob Sapp.  It was the first event under the Invasion Attack name.

NJPW Invasion Tour 2011 | NJPW Invasion Tour 2011: Attack on East Coast was the first American tour run by Japanese professional wrestling promotion New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW) in May 2011.  The tour was hosted by Jersey All Pro Wrestling (JAPW) and featured all of NJPW's top wrestlers, matches for three of the promotion's four championships and the introduction of a new fifth title.

Invasion Attack 2015 | Invasion Attack 2015 was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 5, 2015, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan and featured nine matches, four of which were contested for championships.  In addition to airing worldwide through NJPW's internet streaming site, NJPW World, the event also aired in Japan as a regular PPV through SKY PerfecTV! 's Sukachan service.  It was the third event under the Invasion Attack name.

Invasion Attack 2014 | Invasion Attack 2014 was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 6, 2014, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan.  The event featured nine matches, five of which were contested for championships.  It was the second event under the Invasion Attack name.

Invasion Attack 2016 | Invasion Attack 2016 was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW).  The event took place on April 10, 2016, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan, and featured nine matches, six of which were contested for championships.  The main event saw the winner of the 2016 New Japan Cup, Tetsuya Naito defeat Kazuchika Okada for the IWGP Heavyweight Championship.  The event aired worldwide on NJPW World and featured English commentary, provided by Kevin Kelly and Steve Corino.  In addition, the event also aired in Japan as a PPV through SKY PerfecTV! .  Invasion Attack 2016 was the fourth and last event under the Invasion Attack name, before the event was renamed Sakura Genesis in 2017.

Sakura Genesis 2017 | Sakura Genesis 2017 was a professional wrestling event promoted by New Japan Pro-Wrestling (NJPW), which took place on April 9, 2017, in Tokyo at Ryōgoku Kokugikan.  The event featured nine matches (in addition to one dark match), main evented by 2017 New Japan Cup winner Katsuyori Shibata challenging Kazuchika Okada for the IWGP Heavyweight Championship.  Previously held under the Invasion Attack name, this marked the first show under the Sakura Genesis name.

Card (sports) | In sports, a card lists the matches taking place in a title match combat-sport event.  Cards include a main event match and the undercard listing the rest of the matches.  The undercard may be divided into a midcard and a lower card, according to the perceived importance of the matches.  Promoters schedule matches to occur in ascending order of importance.
response.summary_2: Waimānalo, Hawaii. Invasion Attack (2013) featured participation from Akebono, who is from Waimānalo, Hawaii.
